---
title: "Methods"
---

# Study design and setting

This study reports findings from the hearing component of the UK National Eye and Hearing Study (UKNEHS) pilot, a cross-sectional population-based study designed to assess the feasibility of conducting a national sensory epidemiology programme in the United Kingdom. Participants were recruited across three settings: a dedicated research site, assessments conducted in participants’ own homes, and care-home environments.

# Participants

Adults aged 50 years and older were recruited using a combination of community-based approaches and targeted recruitment within care-home settings. Participants were eligible if they were able to undergo hearing assessment and provide informed consent. Individuals with incomplete audiometric data were excluded from the primary hearing analysis.

# Audiometric assessment
Hearing was assessed using air conduction pure-tone audiometry conducted by trained personnel using calibrated, portable audiometric equipment (including TympaHealth and hearX systems), enabling assessment across both clinical and non-clinical environments. Assessments were conducted in accordance with a standardised audiometric protocol across all settings.Testing was conducted in quiet environments using noise-attenuating headphones, with procedures adapted for non-clinical settings to optimise test reliability while maintaining feasibility.
Prior to audiometric testing, otoscopic examination was performed, and obstructing cerumen was removed where necessary to allow accurate assessment.
Air conduction thresholds were obtained at standard audiometric frequencies. The primary outcome measure was the pure-tone average (PTA) across 500, 1000, 2000 and 4000 Hz in the better-hearing ear. Hearing loss was defined as a PTA ≥25 dB HL and categorised according to World Health Organization (WHO) severity thresholds.
Self-reported measures and hearing aid use
Participants provided self-reported information on hearing difficulty using structured questionnaires administered at the time of assessment. Hearing aid use was recorded based on participant self-report..

# Statistical Methods 
All analyses were performed in R 4.5.3 (R Project for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria). Participants with incomplete audiometric data were excluded from the primary analysis. 
Hearing loss was defined using the better-hearing ear pure-tone average (PTA) across 500, 1000, 2000 and 4000 Hz. Hearing loss was defined as a PTA ≥20 dB HL, and severity was classified according to World Health Organization (WHO) hearing loss categories.
Participant characteristics were summarised overall and by hearing status. Continuous variables were described using median and IQR, and categorical variables using frequencies and percentages. Comparisons between groups were performed using Kruskal–Wallis rank-sum tests for continuous variables and Pearson’s χ² tests or Fisher’s exact tests for categorical variables, as appropriate. To account for multiple testing in exploratory univariable analyses, p values were adjusted using correction using false-discovery rate.
Primary analyses were descriptive and examined the prevalence and severity of hearing loss overall and by recruitment setting. Agreement between audiometric hearing loss and self-reported hearing difficulty was also assessed. Hearing aid use was described according to audiometric hearing status and severity. Analyses involving questionnaire-derived variables were restricted to participants with complete data for those measures.
A two-sided p value <0.05 was considered statistically significant, unless a false discovery rate was conducted, in which a q value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.
